_meta.description: Functional requirements for construction-scale 3D printing. Maps building program requirements to structural and manufacturing constraints.

agent_guidance: Single-story residential is the sweet spot for structural printing today. Proven by ICON (100+ homes), COBOD, and others.
IRC 2021 governs residential construction in most US jurisdictions. Know it or partner with someone who does.
Typical flow: pour foundation (2 weeks) → print walls (1-3 days) → install roof (1 week) → MEP + finishes (3-5 weeks).
Wall printing is FAST (1-3 days for a typical home). The bottleneck is everything ELSE — foundation, roof, MEP, finishes.
Design for the printer's reach: gantry printers (ICON Titan, COBOD) have a fixed footprint. Plan the building within it.
Open floor plans reduce interior walls = less print time = lower cost. But some interior bearing walls may be needed for roof support.
Minimize wall penetrations (windows, doors). Each opening requires a header and interrupts the continuous print path.
ICON's Wolf Ranch homes: 1,574-2,000 sqft, single story, $430K-$600K. That's the current market benchmark.
Cost is the PRIMARY driver. Every design decision filters through 'does this reduce $/sqft?'
Minimize geometry complexity — straight walls, minimal curves, few openings, simple roof shape.
Combined living/kitchen saves one interior wall and 20+ sqft of circulation space.
Flat or shed roof is cheapest — eliminates truss engineering and simplifies the roof-to-wall connection.
ADA accessibility is often required for publicly funded housing — 36-inch doors, 5-foot turning radius in bathroom.
ICON's Community First! Village: permanent housing for formerly homeless individuals. Best-known affordable printed housing project.
Target $100/sqft total construction cost. This is aggressive — material + foundation + MEP + finishes must all be optimized.
Cluster designs (multiple units sharing walls) reduce total wall length and foundation cost per unit.
Earth-based materials at $30/m3 could hit affordable targets in developing world contexts where labor costs are lower.
SPEED IS PARAMOUNT. Military values time-to-operational over cost optimization. Design for fastest possible completion.
Repeatable, modular designs that can be deployed across multiple sites with minimal redesign.
Thicker walls (250mm+) for force protection. Concrete inherently resists small arms and fragmentation.
ICON completed 3x 570 sqft barracks at Fort Bliss under ACES program. That's the current benchmark.
Full audit trail required: every layer, every material batch, every quality check documented and traceable.
Design for expeditionary foundation — may not have time for full traditional foundation in forward deployments.
UFC standards govern military construction. Antiterrorism standoff distances and progressive collapse resistance are mandatory.
ICON received $4.7M Army SBIR Phase 2 contract (Jan 2026) — military procurement is actively funding this space.
Kiln's audit trail, safety profiles, and event logging are EXACTLY what military QA/QC requires.
DEPLOYMENT SPEED IS EVERYTHING. People need shelter NOW, not in 4 weeks.
Earth-based materials are ideal when available — $30/m3, local sourcing, minimal supply chain dependency.
Design for the simplest possible geometry — rectangular or circular plan, minimal openings, lightweight roof.
Circular/dome plans resist wind loads better than rectangular. Consider for hurricane-prone regions.
Must withstand aftershocks (earthquake) or subsequent storms (hurricane). Design for the NEXT disaster, not just the current one.
Culturally appropriate design matters. A shelter that people won't use is worthless. Research local building traditions.
Design for permanent upgrade path — initial shelter becomes the core of a permanent home with added rooms/finishes over time.
Minimal foundation: grade beam or rubble trench. Can't wait weeks for traditional foundation in emergency context.
Power: design for generator operation. Grid power may not exist. Solar-powered pump systems are emerging.
Commercial buildings need larger clear spans than residential. Steel roof structure on printed walls is typical.
IBC 2021 governs (not IRC). More stringent fire, structural, and accessibility requirements.
Storefront glazing: large openings require engineered steel headers. Plan wall layout around glazing requirements.
ADA is mandatory: accessible entrance, restrooms, circulation paths, parking.
Fire-rated walls: printed concrete inherently achieves 2-4 hour fire rating. Document and certify per code.
ASHRAE 90.1 for energy code — more demanding than residential IECC. Continuous insulation typically required.
Open floor plans benefit from printed exterior walls + steel interior columns. Don't try to span 15m with printed walls alone.
